claim: The four-level prompt framework (ad-hoc → reusable → templated with examples → production code) scales prompts from one-off experiments to infinitely scalable applications
actionable: Classify your prompts by level: Level 1 (ad-hoc), Level 2 (static variables), Level 3 (examples), Level 4 (dynamic variables in code). Move up levels as prompts mature and become reusable
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: XML-structured prompts outperform free-form natural language prompts on all models because LLMs trained on HTML (XML subset) understand and follow XML-based instructions more reliably
actionable: Structure all production prompts using XML tags with sections for <purpose>, <instructions>, <examples>, and dynamic <variables>; avoid unstructured prose for consistent results
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: Level 3 prompts add concrete examples to guide output format; examples steer the model more effectively than instructions alone, showing exactly how you want responses formatted
actionable: When you need consistent output formatting, include 2-3 concrete examples showing the exact structure, tone, and fields you expect in the response
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: Level 4 prompts use dynamic variables (replaced at runtime) to make prompts infinitely scalable; every non-chatbot AI application is effectively a Level 4 prompt embedded in code
actionable: Extract static instructions and examples into a template file with placeholder variables; replace them at runtime from application data to create production-grade prompt-based tools
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: Using CLI tools like `llm` and `ollama` enables rapid ad-hoc prompt prototyping across multiple models without switching between chat interfaces or losing prompt versions
actionable: Create prompt.txt files and test them via CLI: `llm -m model_name < prompt.txt`; iterate locally before wrapping in code or storing in a prompt library
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: Prompt libraries (e.g., built with Marimo) allow fast retrieval and execution of reusable Level 3/4 prompts with dynamic variable filling, reducing context switching and prompt duplication
actionable: Build a personal prompt library tool (using Marimo or similar) that stores XML-structured prompts with variable slots; query and execute them with dynamic data to scale productivity
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: The prompt is the fundamental unit of knowledge work in the generative AI age; mastering prompt engineering is more valuable than mastering frameworks like LangChain or AutoGen
actionable: Stay close to the metal: use simple CLI tools and direct prompt management instead of over-engineered frameworks; invest time in prompt quality over framework complexity
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM

claim: Lower-tier local models (Llama, Qwen) improve instruction adherence dramatically when prompts are structured (Level 2+); unstructured prompts cause them to fail while structured prompts succeed
actionable: For local models, always use XML-structured prompts with clear sections, instructions, and examples; ad-hoc prompts will fail while structured ones succeed
source_title: Prompt Engineering Master Class for ENGINEERS with Ollama and LLM
